item.title: “Monks, the large deep lake refers to sensual pleasures. Monks, the great herd of deer refers to beings. Monks, the man who does not desire…
item.description:
“Monks, the large deep lake refers to sensual pleasures. Monks, the great herd of deer refers to beings. Monks, the man who does not desire their welfare, benefit, security refers to Māra the Evil One. Monks, the dangerous path refers to the wrong eightfold path – wrong view, wrong intention, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong concentration. Monks, the male decoy refers to delight and greed. Monks, the female decoy refers to ignorance. Monks, the man who desires their welfare, benefit, security refers to the Tathāgata, the Arahant, the Perfectly Enlightened One. The safe, secure path leading to happiness refers to the Noble Eightfold Path – right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 19 – Dvedhāvitakkasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Eraka: “O Eraka, painful are sensual pleasures! O Eraka, not happiness are sensual pleasures! One who desires sensual pleasures, desires suffering. One who does…
item.description:
Elder Eraka:
“O Eraka, painful are sensual pleasures!
O Eraka, not happiness are sensual pleasures!
One who desires sensual pleasures, desires suffering.
One who does not desire sensual pleasures, does not desire suffering.
O Eraka, know this as it is.”

From: Theragāthā 1.93
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - “What do you think, Mālukyaputta? Regarding forms cognizable by the eye that you have not seen, never saw before, do not see now, and…
item.description:
- “What do you think, Mālukyaputta? Regarding forms cognizable by the eye that you have not seen, never saw before, do not see now, and do not wish to see — do you have any desire, lust, or craving for them?” - “No, venerable sir.” - “Regarding sounds cognizable by the ear … odors cognizable by the nose … tastes cognizable by the tongue … touches cognizable by the body that you have not felt, never felt before, do not feel now, and do not wish to feel — do you have any desire, lust, or craving for them?” - “No, venerable sir.”

Seeing a form, if one’s mind is infatuated,
Attending to a pleasing sign,
The mind is attached to the feeling,
Clinging to the form remains.
His feelings increase,
Manyfold, born from form —
Greed and hurtful thoughts
Harm his mind.
Thus accumulating suffering,
He is said to be far from Nibbāna.

Hearing a sound, mind infatuated …
Smelling an odor …
Tasting a flavor …
If one’s mind is infatuated,
Attending to a pleasing sign,
The mind attached to the feeling,
Clinging to the flavor remains.
His feelings increase,
Manyfold, born from flavor —
Greed and hurtful thoughts
Harm his mind.
Thus accumulating suffering,
He is said to be far from Nibbāna.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.95
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Venerable sir, thus a person has a stain and truly knows: ‘There is a stain in me.’ For such a person, it can be expected:…
item.description:
“Venerable sir, thus a person has a stain and truly knows: ‘There is a stain in me.’ For such a person, it can be expected: ‘He will arouse desire, make effort, arouse energy to abandon that stain. He will die without greed, without ill-will, without delusion, without stain, his mind unpolluted.’”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 5 – Anaṅgaṇasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “I do not see any other thing, monks, that so gives rise to unarisen sensual desire, or to the increase and expansion of arisen sensual…
item.description:
“I do not see any other thing, monks, that so gives rise to unarisen sensual desire, or to the increase and expansion of arisen sensual desire, as the sign of the beautiful. The sign of the beautiful, monks, when unwisely attended to, gives rise to unarisen sensual desire and increases and expands arisen sensual desire.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 1.11
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Householder, just as a house with a badly thatched roof — the ridgepole is not protected, the rafters are not protected, the walls are not…
item.description:
“Householder, just as a house with a badly thatched roof — the ridgepole is not protected, the rafters are not protected, the walls are not protected; the ridgepole becomes soaked and leaks, the rafters become soaked and leak, the walls become soaked and leak; the ridgepole rots, the rafters rot, the walls rot. So too, householder, when the mind is not guarded, bodily actions are not guarded, verbal actions … mental actions … then bodily actions become soaked with greed and leak, verbal actions become soaked with greed and leak, mental actions become soaked with greed and leak. For one whose bodily actions are soaked and leak with greed, whose verbal and mental actions are soaked and leak with greed, bodily actions rot, verbal actions rot, mental actions rot. For one whose bodily, verbal, and mental actions rot, death is not good, the end is not good.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 3.109
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Samiddhatta: “The five aggregates are fully understood, Their root is cut off. The wandering of birth is ended, Now there is no further becoming.”
item.description:
Elder Samiddhatta:
“The five aggregates are fully understood,
Their root is cut off.
The wandering of birth is ended,
Now there is no further becoming.”

From: Theragāthā 1.90
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Carpenter, what are wholesome thoughts? Renunciation thought, non-ill-will thought, harmlessness thought. These, Carpenter, are called wholesome thoughts.”
item.description:
“Carpenter, what are wholesome thoughts? Renunciation thought, non-ill-will thought, harmlessness thought. These, Carpenter, are called wholesome thoughts.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 78 – Samaṇamuṇḍikasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Sitting down to one side, that monk said to the Venerable Sāriputta: “Venerable Sāriputta, a monk who is my companion has abandoned the training and…
item.description:
Sitting down to one side, that monk said to the Venerable Sāriputta: “Venerable Sāriputta, a monk who is my companion has abandoned the training and returned to the lower life.” - “That comes to be, friend, for one who does not guard the sense faculties, does not know moderation in eating, and is not committed to wakefulness. For such a person, it is impossible that he would continue the perfectly pure holy life for his whole life. Friend, a monk who does guard the sense faculties, knows moderation in eating, and is committed to wakefulness — it is possible that he would continue the perfectly pure holy life for his whole life. Therefore, friend, you should train thus: ‘We will guard the sense faculties, know moderation in eating, and be committed to wakefulness.’ Thus, friend, you should train.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.120
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, it is because of not realizing, not penetrating four things that you and I have wandered for such a long time in this cycle…
item.description:
“Monks, it is because of not realizing, not penetrating four things that you and I have wandered for such a long time in this cycle of birth and death. What four? Monks, because of not realizing, not penetrating noble virtue, you and I have wandered for such a long time in this cycle of birth and death. Monks, because of not realizing, not penetrating noble concentration … noble wisdom … noble liberation, you and I have wandered for such a long time in this cycle of birth and death.”

From: Dīgha Nikāya 16 – Mahāparinibbānasutta
📖 Read this sutta
